abstract:
This article provides a fresh exegetical analysis of Romans 8:3–4, focusing on how Paul frames the resolution of sin and its systemic complications. By reevaluating the syntactic "awkwardness" of the opening clause (τὸ ... ἀδύνατον), the author argues that it functions as an accusative of respect rather than an anacoluthon. This grammatical shift clarifies that Paul is specifically addressing a threefold problem inherited from the discourse in Romans 7:7–25: the dominant power of personified sin, the inherent weakness of human flesh, and the resulting incapability of the law to effect righteousness. 

The study demonstrates that God’s intervention through the mission of His Son provides a comprehensive solution to this impasse. By sending Christ in the "likeness of sinful flesh," God was able to condemn sin within the very sphere where it held sway. The analysis concludes that this divine action not only breaks the authority of sin but also resolves the law's inability to bring life, as Christ’s work fulfills the law's requirements for those now living according to the Spirit. Through this lens, Romans 8:3–4 emerges as a pivotal transition from the frustration of the old epoch to the participatory freedom found in the new Christological reality.
